executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment continues its post-pandemic normalization trajectory, with 75.3 million enrollees across 51 states as of January 2026, representing widespread year-over-year declines as states complete redetermination processes. Most major states experienced enrollment reductions ranging from 1.8% (New York) to 7.6% (Michigan), with only North Carolina posting modest growth at 0.5%. National per-enrollee spending reached $2,869 in fiscal year 2024, though significant state variation persists, ranging from Florida's efficient $2,031 per enrollee to Pennsylvania's $4,377. The federal government maintained its standard cost-sharing relationship, contributing $142 billion of the total $216 billion in national Medicaid expenditures.

Texas demonstrates mixed performance relative to national benchmarks and peer states. With 4.03 million enrollees, Texas experienced a moderate 3.1% enrollment decline, matching Pennsylvania's reduction rate while outperforming larger declines in California (6.2%) and Illinois (6.2%). However, Texas's quality performance remains concerning, scoring 62 out of 100 and ranking 30th nationally—significantly below top-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80), though avoiding the bottom tier occupied by southern peers like Arkansas (54) and Alabama (52). Texas maintains strong managed care penetration at 93.3%, well above the national average of 83.2%, with nearly 3.9 million enrollees in managed care arrangements. Per-enrollee costs of $2,988 position Texas near the national average, suggesting reasonable cost efficiency despite quality challenges that warrant strategic attention in upcoming policy cycles.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the third-largest Medicaid program nationally with 4.03 million enrollees, representing 5.4% of the national Medicaid population despite serving the nation's second-most populous state. This enrollment reflects the state's non-expansion status, limiting coverage primarily to children, pregnant women, and disabled individuals. Texas's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, aligning closely with the national unwinding trend and performing better than peer states like Illinois (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%). However, when compared to expansion state North Carolina, which showed slight growth (+0.5%), Texas's contraction highlights the ongoing coverage gap for low-income adults. The state's total Medicaid expenditure of $12.0 billion ranks fourth nationally, with per-enrollee spending of $2,988—slightly above the national average of $2,869 but significantly below expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Ohio ($3,561).

Texas demonstrates operational strengths in managed care implementation, with 93.3% penetration rate exceeding the national average of 83.2%, indicating effective care coordination infrastructure. However, quality performance remains a critical challenge, with Texas scoring 62/100 and ranking 30th nationally—below the national average of 65.6 but ahead of regional peers like Arkansas (54) and Oklahoma (51). This moderate performance suggests room for improvement in care outcomes and member experience. Key areas to monitor include the state's response to federal HCBS quality measure requirements emerging in 2028, ongoing procurement activities through HHSC that may signal program expansions or reforms, and whether Texas will reconsider Medicaid expansion as enrollment pressures mount and quality benchmarks tighten. The state's relatively controlled per-enrollee costs combined with high managed care penetration provide a foundation for quality improvements, but systematic reform may be necessary to achieve top-tier performance.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 significantly exceeds the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: Five States Show Critically Low Quality Scores
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55/100 on quality measures, well below the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Achieves Excellence in Quality Despite High Costs
alerts.description: Massachusetts leads with 81/100 quality score while maintaining $4,201 per-enrollee spending, demonstrating effective high-investment care model.

alerts.title: Florida Shows Concerning Quality-Cost Gap
alerts.description: Florida has the lowest per-enrollee spending at $2,031 but quality data is missing, raising concerns about potential underinvestment in care.

alerts.title: Texas Moderate Performance Despite High Managed Care Rate
alerts.description: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration (above 83.2% national average) but only moderate quality score of 62/100, suggesting managed care efficiency issues.

executiveInsights.title: Texas underperforming on quality metrics at rank #30 of 50 states
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62/100 on Medicaid quality measures, ranking 30th nationally and falling into the 'moderate' tier. This is significantly below the national average of 65.6 and well below high performers scoring 75+.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Poor quality scores can trigger CMS scrutiny and impact contract renewals. This creates immediate risk for the HHSC contract and opportunity for Accenture to position quality improvement services.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement opportunities to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas Medicaid enrollment declining 3.1% YoY with 4M+ enrollees at risk
executiveInsights.summary: Texas lost 3.1% of Medicaid enrollees year-over-year, representing a significant population decline from its 4,032,864 total enrollees. This follows the national trend of enrollment decreases post-pandemic.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines directly impact contract value and HHSC operational planning. Accenture needs to help HHSC prepare for continued rightsizing of operations and potential budget adjustments.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment forecasting models and operational capacity planning with HHSC

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration at 93.3% exceeds national average of 83.2%
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 3,863,808 enrollees in managed care representing 93.3% penetration, significantly above the national average of 83.2%. This indicates advanced managed care infrastructure and operations.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration suggests sophisticated operations that require specialized technology and oversight capabilities. This validates Accenture's managed care expertise as core to the contract value.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care oversight capabilities and technology requirements with HHSC

executiveInsights.title: New CMS HCBS quality measures for 2028 will impact Texas operations
executiveInsights.summary: CMS announced new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set for 2028, creating upcoming regulatory requirements. Texas will need to adapt its quality measurement and reporting systems.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures require system updates and process changes, creating expansion opportunities for Accenture's contract scope and demonstrating ongoing regulatory compliance needs.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag upcoming HCBS quality measure requirements to HHSC compliance team

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs at $2,988 below national average of $2,869
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, with total expenditures of $12.048 billion. This is significantly lower than comparable large states like NY ($3,431) and PA ($4,377).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Lower per-enrollee costs suggest either efficient operations or potential underinvestment in services. This cost efficiency creates pressure to maintain lean operations while meeting quality targets.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost efficiency benchmarking analysis with HHSC finance team

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline impact
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY, indicating post-PHE unwinding effects that could reduce contract values and require system adjustments.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop flexible capacity models to manage enrollment volatility and maintain cost efficiency.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating vulnerability for performance-based contracts and CMS oversight.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive quality improvement initiative targeting specific measure gaps to move Texas into top-tier performance.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Medicaid HCBS quality measure set creates compliance requirements and potential contract modifications.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position as early adopter for HCBS quality measurement and reporting capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration suggests potential upcoming MCO contract recompetitions requiring systems support.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal and prepare comprehensive managed care technology solutions for anticipated RFPs.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas per-enrollee costs at $2,988 are above national average, creating pressure for cost containment initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop analytics-driven cost containment strategies and predictive modeling capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight risk
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio and work plan items indicate heightened federal scrutiny of state programs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring and audit preparation capabilities across all Texas contracts.

riskOpportunity.label: Data collection expansion
riskOpportunity.description: New CMS generic information collection activities for Medicaid and CHIP create opportunities for data management solutions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop standardized data collection and reporting platforms aligned with emerging CMS requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: Procurement pipeline access
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ESBD and HHSC procurement portals provide early visibility into upcoming contracting opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish systematic monitoring of Texas procurement channels with automated opportunity alerts.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment decreased 3.1% year-over-year to 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, reflecting post-pandemic normalization trends.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee annually, slightly above the national average of $2,869.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees, well above the national average of 83.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a moderate quality score of 62, below the national average of 65.6.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement portals are being monitored for Medicaid-related opportunities and contracts.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Six federal regulatory and OIG items are currently active that may impact Texas Medicaid operations.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific upcoming milestones are identified in the available data for Texas Medicaid.
